headline: Atlassian to Reduce 1,600 Jobs in the Latest AI-Linked Cuts
summary: Australian billionaire founder Mike Cannon-Brookes explained the reductions in a staff memo, while also announcing his chief technology officer was leaving the Sydney-based company.  The reductions are the latest AI-linked cuts in the software industry and beyond, as companies across the globe adapt to an era in which the technology can handle many of the tasks thus far performed by humans.  “It would be disingenuous to pretend AI doesn’t change the mix of skills we need or the number of roles required in certain areas,” Cannon-Brookes said.

content.title: Atlassian to Reduce 1,600 Jobs in the Latest AI-Linked Cuts
content.summary: Australian billionaire founder Mike Cannon-Brookes explained the reductions in a staff memo, while also announcing his chief technology officer was leaving the Sydney-based company.  The reductions are the latest AI-linked cuts in the software industry and beyond, as companies across the globe adapt to an era in which the technology can handle many of the tasks thus far performed by humans.  “It would be disingenuous to pretend AI doesn’t change the mix of skills we need or the number of roles required in certain areas,” Cannon-Brookes said.

headline: Technically Speaking, Stocks Look Vulnerable
summary: The dreaded Hindenburg Omen, a technical indicator that has preceded stock market swoons, flashed several times in January and February.  Sure enough, the S&P 500 had a 5% peak-to-trough drawdown in two weeks and the market’s so-called fear gauge, the VIX, jumped Monday to its highest since last year’s “Liberation Day” scare.  Unlike some anonymous Polymarket traders, though, the omen named for the doomed German airship couldn’t have known bombs were about to fall on Iran.

content.title: Technically Speaking, Stocks Look Vulnerable
content.summary: The dreaded Hindenburg Omen, a technical indicator that has preceded stock market swoons, flashed several times in January and February.  Sure enough, the S&P 500 had a 5% peak-to-trough drawdown in two weeks and the market’s so-called fear gauge, the VIX, jumped Monday to its highest since last year’s “Liberation Day” scare.  Unlike some anonymous Polymarket traders, though, the omen named for the doomed German airship couldn’t have known bombs were about to fall on Iran.

headline: More Troubled Waters: Heard on the Street Wednesday Recap
summary: Oil prices jumped again, shrugging off a record-sized release from strategic reserves.  Attacks on three commercial ships by Iranian forces in the Strait of Hormuz highlighted that the conflict is far from over.  The Nasdaq composite rose slightly for a second day, due to a pickup in technology stocks.

content.title: More Troubled Waters: Heard on the Street Wednesday Recap
content.summary: Oil prices jumped again, shrugging off a record-sized release from strategic reserves.  Attacks on three commercial ships by Iranian forces in the Strait of Hormuz highlighted that the conflict is far from over.  The Nasdaq composite rose slightly for a second day, due to a pickup in technology stocks.

headline: City of Miami Modernizes Regulatory Services and Accelerates AI-Driven Innovation with Oracle
summary: The City of Miami has chosen Oracle Permitting and Licensing (OPAL) to improve public service delivery, and simplify processes for local residents and businesses. This selection expands on Miami's previous adoption of Oracle Fusion Cloud Applications for finance and HR, equipping the city with an integrated cloud platform for essential business processes.

headline: Oracle Named a Leader in the 2025-2026 IDC MarketScape Report for Worldwide AI-Enabled Embedded Trade Financing Applications
summary: Oracle has been recognized as a leader in the IDC MarketScape: Worldwide AI-Enabled Embedded Trade Financing Applications 2025-2026 Vendor Assessment (doc # US52980825, December 2025). According to the report, businesses should "Consider Oracle when your organization requires a robust, scalable solution to digitize, unify, and automate trade finance operations powered by AI across geographies and complex product types."

headline: Software companies fight back against fears that AI will kill them
summary: Oracle's Mike Sicilia is the latest software CEO to wade in to the debate on whether artificial intelligence tools that heavily automate human tasks will mean the demise of his industry.  "You've all heard ... that new companies coding quickly using AI will spell the death of SaaS (software as a service)," he told analysts on a ‌conference call on Tuesday.  Sicilia was responding to Wall Street concerns that new AI tools can now perform some of the tasks that traditional software companies' products were built for, such as organizing customer information or guiding people through business processes.

content.title: Software companies fight back against fears that AI will kill them
content.summary: Oracle's Mike Sicilia is the latest software CEO to wade in to the debate on whether artificial intelligence tools that heavily automate human tasks will mean the demise of his industry.  "You've all heard ... that new companies coding quickly using AI will spell the death of SaaS (software as a service)," he told analysts on a ‌conference call on Tuesday.  Sicilia was responding to Wall Street concerns that new AI tools can now perform some of the tasks that traditional software companies' products were built for, such as organizing customer information or guiding people through business processes.
